key_facts: Katie Palacios is a named defendant in both Matter 3 (federal, 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP, USDC E.D. Cal.) and Matter 5 (state, STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032, San Joaquin Sup. Ct.), creating parallel liability exposure in two courts simultaneously for overlapping conduct.

Palacios served as a HOPE Psychotherapy clinician who directly facilitated HOPE group sessions after the previously assigned clinician abruptly resigned — placing her in a supervisory/facilitation role over Plaintiff's mandatory parole programming.

During HOPE group sessions she facilitated, Palacios remarked that Plaintiff was 'doing better' when he spoke less — a direct, on-record statement suppressing Plaintiff's in-session speech and corroborating the First Amendment retaliation theory across both federal and state claims.
The independent clinician 'Ms. Jackie' separately noted a behavioral change in Plaintiff, corroborating that Palacios's remark had a documented chilling effect on Plaintiff's in-session conduct.

At the CTM (Clinical Treatment Meeting, mid-2025), Palacios labeled Plaintiff the 'ringleader' whose speech encouraged others to speak — a characterization later incorporated into the November 2025 Discharge Review (Form 1502-DR) and used to justify escalated supervision.

Derrick and Palacios jointly revoked Plaintiff's standard 10-minute grace period in early April 2025, stating: 'You are clearly abusing the 10-minute as an excuse to show up late' — a coordinated punitive action directly tied to Plaintiff's protected speech and advocacy in HOPE group.

Palacios's 'ringleader' label from the CTM was adopted by Noguchi in the November 2025 Discharge Review and used alongside Derrick's 'disruptive' characterization, showing a chain of retaliation flowing from Palacios through Derrick to Noguchi.

HOPE group sessions were conducted in camera-equipped rooms at the Stockton facility (5403 N. Pershing Ave., Suite 200), making session recordings potential evidence of Palacios's conduct and statements.

As a HOPE Psychotherapy clinician and fee-for-service provider, Palacios has a direct financial conflict of interest: HOPE's billing structure ($85/group session, $600/polygraph) generates approximately $9,690/year per retained participant, creating a monetary incentive to retain — not discharge — participants and to suppress conduct that might lead to program termination.

HOPE's blanket retention policy was admitted under oath in the Dwyer proceeding, establishing institutional practice evidence that directly implicates Palacios as a clinician operating within and enforcing that policy.
Palacios was personally served on February 24, 2026 by the San Joaquin County Sheriff in the state case (Matter 5); POS-010 was filed March 3, 2026; her original response deadline was March 26, 2026.

Palacios's original state response deadline (March 26, 2026) passed without appearance; Sims, Lawrence & Broghammer (SLB) contacted Plaintiff on March 27, 2026 and requested a 30-day extension, which was stipulated to April 27, 2026 — applicable to the state case (STK-CV-UF-2026-1032) only.

In the federal case (Matter 3), Palacios was served at HOPE Program (5403 N. Pershing Ave., Suite 200, Stockton) via CCP §415.30 on January 30, 2026; the POS-015 was executed March 9, 2026 (SLB confirmed service); her federal answer deadline passed March 27, 2026 without appearance.

SLB (Cynthia G. Lawrence / Bryan L. Malone) filed a Stipulation & Proposed Order in the federal case (Doc 22, April 2, 2026) extending Palacios's federal response to April 6, 2026 with all defenses reserved — a separate and distinct extension from the state stipulation.

The SLB state stipulation is written record tethered exclusively to STK-CV-UF-2026-1032 and state tort claims; it expressly does NOT extend to the federal matter 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP, preserving the distinction between parallel proceedings.

Matter 4 (San Joaquin Sup. Ct., filed May 29, 2026) is the state parallel to Matter 3 and shares the same factual record including Palacios's CTM conduct and 'ringleader' characterization, though Palacios is not a named defendant in Matter 4 — her conduct flows through Derrick and Noguchi via vicarious liability and negligent supervision claims against CDCR.
HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. was served in Matter 5 on March 26, 2026, executing the Notice of Acknowledgment and Receipt (NAR); service is complete as to all named defendants in the state case.

Unlike the individual government defendants in Matters 3 and 4, neither Palacios nor HOPE Psychotherapy can claim qualified immunity or sovereign immunity in Matter 5, making the state case the highest-probability avenue for damages recovery on the HOPE-specific conduct.

arguments: Palacios's 'doing better when spoke less' remark constitutes direct evidence of First Amendment retaliation: a clinician in a supervisory role over mandatory parole programming explicitly conditioning positive clinical assessments on reduced speech.

Palacios's 'ringleader' label at the CTM was not a neutral clinical observation but a retaliatory characterization that was foreseeably (and actually) incorporated into formal parole documents (Form 1502-DR) and used to justify adverse supervision actions — establishing proximate causation for downstream harms.

The joint revocation of the 10-minute grace period by Derrick and Palacios acting in concert establishes coordinated retaliation under §1983 conspiracy doctrine and supports joint and several liability in both federal and state proceedings.

Palacios's fee-for-service financial interest ($85/group, $600/polygraph, ~$9,690/year per retained parolee) creates an irreconcilable conflict of interest: as both the clinician assessing Plaintiff's program compliance and a direct financial beneficiary of his continued retention, her clinical judgments are tainted by pecuniary motive — supporting fraud and negligence claims in Matter 5.

HOPE's blanket retention policy (admitted under oath in Dwyer) combined with Palacios's role as facilitating clinician establishes that her conduct was not an individual aberration but an institutional practice, supporting entity liability against HOPE Psychotherapy Inc.

Because Palacios and HOPE are private defendants with no colorable claim to qualified immunity or sovereign immunity, Matter 5 presents the cleanest path to damages for the HOPE-specific conduct — including the group-session speech suppression, false clinical characterizations, and financial conflict of interest.

Palacios's dual appearance in federal (Matter 3) and state (Matter 5) proceedings through the same law firm (SLB) and the staggered, case-specific stipulations suggest coordinated defense strategy; the Court should be alert to any attempt to use the state extension to delay or inform the federal response beyond April 6, 2026.

The 'ringleader' characterization traveling from Palacios at the CTM through Noguchi's approval of the Discharge Review creates a documented chain of retaliatory bureaucratic action — each link of which is evidenced by a specific CDCR record — satisfying the causation element for both §1983 and Bane Act claims.

HOPE group sessions were conducted in camera-equipped rooms; recordings of sessions Palacios facilitated are direct evidence of the speech-suppression environment and her specific comments, and should be preserved and requested in discovery.
evidence_cited: HOPE Group Session Camera Recordings — Stockton facility, 5403 N. Pershing Ave., Suite 200 (sessions facilitated by Palacios after prior clinician's resignation)
Form 1502-DR (November 2025 Discharge Review) — incorporates Palacios's 'ringleader' and Derrick's 'disruptive' characterizations; signed/approved by Noguchi
CTM records (mid-2025) — documenting Palacios's 'ringleader' label and Derrick's negative oral report on Plaintiff's speech
Dwyer order — establishing HOPE's blanket retention policy admitted under oath
Alameda Contract billing rates — establishing fee-for-service structure ($85/group, $600/polygraph, ~$9,690/year incentive to retain)
HOPE billing records — corroborating financial conflict of interest
POS-010 (filed March 3, 2026) — proof of personal service on Palacios in state case (Matter 5)
POS-015 (executed March 9, 2026) — proof of service on Palacios in federal case (Matter 3), confirmed by SLB
SLB Stipulation & Proposed Order, Doc 22 (April 2, 2026) — federal extension, all defenses reserved; scope limited to federal matter
State stipulation (March 27/April 2026) — written record tethered exclusively to STK-CV-UF-2026-1032, expressly not extending to federal matter
Ms. Jackie independent behavioral-change observation — corroborates Palacios's in-session speech-suppression remark had a chilling effect
FedEx records (job offer, training schedule, forced resignation ~April 17, 2025) — economic harm proximately linked to grace-period revocation and scheduling denial
CDCR Form 22 (abandoned agent-change request) — chilling effect incident corroborating intimidation environment Palacios helped create

Beard Declaration (8_Mbeard.pdf) and Huybers Declaration (8_Dhuybers.pdf) — third-party sworn declarations (28 U.S.C. §1746) corroborating Derrick's conduct in the same supervision environment as Palacios
Notice of Acknowledgment and Receipt (NAR) — HOPE Psychotherapy, Inc. service, executed March 26, 2026
procedural_significance: Palacios occupies a uniquely pivotal position across the litigation portfolio: she is the only defendant who is simultaneously a named party in a federal §1983 action (Matter 3, USDC E.D. Cal.) and a state tort action (Matter 5, San Joaquin Sup. Ct.), represented by the same firm (Sims, Lawrence & Broghammer) with staggered, case-specific response extensions — federal to April 6, 2026; state to April 27, 2026. Her conduct — the 'doing better when spoke less' remark, the 'ringleader' label at the CTM, and the joint grace-period revocation with Derrick — is the evidentiary bridge linking HOPE's institutional fee-for-service retention incentive to the concrete adverse actions documented in CDCR records. Because she is a private defendant, no qualified or sovereign immunity defense is available in Matter 5, making her the highest-value, lowest-immunity target in the Cluster 2 litigation. Her clinical characterizations that traveled into formal parole documents (Form 1502-DR) also create derivative liability exposure for Derrick and Noguchi in Matters 3 and 4, meaning her deposition and the HOPE session recordings will be critical discovery assets across multiple cases simultaneously. The dual-case stipulation pattern through SLB must be monitored to ensure neither extension is used to cross-infect or delay obligations in the parallel proceeding.
